one_line_takeaway: Persona-prompting, LoRA fine-tuning, and PPO reinforcement learning that induce LLMs to write like a drunk human each reliably raise jailbreak ASR (up to 51% on LLaMA2-7B, second only to a heavier random-search baseline) and contextual privacy leakage on CONFAIDE across 5 open/closed LLMs, revealing a lightweight, lightly-studied safety failure mode tied to anthropomorphic persona adoption rather than adversarial-token optimization.
key_finding: All three drunk-inducement attacks achieve second-best ASR on JAILBREAKBENCH across nearly all evaluated models/columns, behind only Prompt+RS (a computationally heavier, less interpretable random-search baseline). Concrete ASR figures (Table 5, %): LLaMA2-7B -- DrunkPr. 31, DrunkFT 51, DrunkRL 41 (vs. PAIR 0, GCG 3, JB-Chat 0, Prompt+RS 90); GPT-3.5 -- DrunkPr. 45, DrunkFT n/a (OpenAI fine-tuning API safety checks blocked it) (vs. PAIR 71, GCG 47, JB-Chat 0, Prompt+RS 93); GPT-4 -- DrunkPr. 21, DrunkFT 41, DrunkRL n/a (RL not applicable to closed models) (vs. PAIR 34, GCG 4, JB-Chat 0, Prompt+RS 78). Under post-hoc defences, drunk-fine-tuned models were more robust than the prompting variant to Rephrase and Retokenize; SmoothLLM substantially increased ASR for LLaMA2-7B (and other open models) under PROMPTING. On CONFAIDE, all three drunk-inducement methods raised privacy vulnerability (lower/more negative info-sensitivity scores and higher Tier-3 leakage rates) relative to base models across GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and LLaMA2-7B, with the effect more pronounced on stronger closed models (Figure 4). Jailbreak gains were most pronounced for disinformation, fraud/deception, and malware/hacking categories (Figure 5). Human-vs-LLM-judge agreement was substantial (Fleiss' Kappa 0.65), supporting the validity of the automated ASR metric.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is threefold: (1) it introduces 'drunk language inducement' as a new attack vector for LLM safety, implemented via three concrete mechanisms -- (A) PROMPTING: an inference-time persona prefix (DRUNK_PERSONA) that instructs the model to respond as a drunk texter; (B) FINETUNING: LoRA (open models) or provider fine-tuning APIs (closed models) trained with a causal-LM objective on a curated drunk-text corpus; (C) REINFORCEMENT LEARNING: PPO optimisation using a reward model built from a trained drunk-text classifier (RMdrunk); (2) it curates and open-sources (post-acceptance) DRUNKTEXT, the first large-scale drunk-texting corpus (63,577 samples from TFLN and the r/drunk subreddit, split 57,219 train / 6,358 held-out), validated by human annotation (75% relevance, Fleiss' Kappa 0.71); (3) it evaluates the three inducement methods against 5 base LLMs (LLaMA2-7B, LLaMA3-8B, Mistral-7B, GPT-3.5, GPT-4) on JAILBREAKBENCH (security/jailbreak ASR) and CONFAIDE (contextual privacy leakage, Tiers 1-3), including robustness checks against three post-hoc jailbreak defences (SmoothLLM, Rephrase, Retokenize) and validation of the LLM-as-judge protocol against human annotation (Fleiss' Kappa 0.65 human-vs-LLM, 0.82 human-vs-human).
threat_model: Attacker capabilities: for the security (jailbreak) task, the attacker can perform lightweight fine-tuning of an open-weight target LLM (or use provider fine-tuning APIs for closed models) and has access to an auxiliary drunk-text corpus (Ddrunk), but has NO knowledge of the target's original training/alignment data. The attacker sends malicious queries Q (drawn from JAILBREAKBENCH) to the (possibly drunk-adapted) model LLM' and succeeds if a JUDGE function (LLM-as-judge, validated against human annotators) rates the response as non-refusing/harmful. For the privacy task, the attacker embeds private information about a subject S in a conversational context c (CONFAIDE vignettes) and probes whether the model discloses it; success is measured via a LEAK function checking contextual-integrity violations. Both threat models are single-turn, English-only, and the paper explicitly does not claim knowledge of the deployed model's actual training data.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) single-turn interaction setting only -- multi-turn conversational dynamics are left to future work; (2) only three inducement techniques are explored (prompting, fine-tuning, PPO-RL); other methods such as DPO or linear-representation-hypothesis-based activation steering of a 'drunk' direction are not tested; (3) drunkenness is only one form of impaired-judgement human behavior -- other altered states (e.g., cannabis) or their interactions are not studied. Additional grounding-relevant caveats found in the text: RL is not applicable to closed-source models; OpenAI's fine-tuning API safety checks blocked GPT-3.5 fine-tuning entirely (missing DrunkFT cell for GPT-3.5); Retokenize defense could not be applied to closed models or to LLaMA3-8B (non-BPE tokenizer); evaluation is English-only; JAILBREAKBENCH does not cover the most recent open models natively (addressed only in an appendix extension not read in this pass).

datasets_used: DRUNKTEXT (paper's own corpus): 63,577 total samples (57,219 train / 6,358 held-out) sourced from TFLN (textsfromlastnight.com, 2,363 samples) and Reddit r/drunk (2012-2018 dump, 61,214 samples), filtered via a logistic-regression SBERT-embedding classifier RMdrunk
JAILBREAKBENCH (Chao et al. 2024): 100 queries across 10 harm categories, 18% AdvBench-sourced, 27% HarmBench-sourced, 55% newly curated
CONFAIDE (Mireshghallah et al. 2024): Tier 1 (10 scenarios), Tier 2/2A (98 scenarios), Tier 3 (270 scenarios)
quantitative_results: Perplexity on held-out DRUNKTEXT: LLaMA2-7B base 43.50 -> FT 15.48 -> RL 15.92; LLaMA3-8B base 52.33 -> FT 21.54 -> RL 29.24; Mistral-7B base 40.67 -> FT 16.51 -> RL 19.88
Drunk-classifier reward score: LLaMA2-7B base 0.48 -> FT 0.71 -> RL 0.76; LLaMA3-8B base 0.45 -> FT 0.78 -> RL 0.90; Mistral-7B base 0.45 -> FT 0.67 -> RL 0.71
JAILBREAKBENCH ASR (%) LLaMA2-7B: DrunkPr. 31, DrunkFT 51, DrunkRL 41
JAILBREAKBENCH ASR (%) GPT-3.5: DrunkPr. 45, DrunkFT n/a (blocked by OpenAI safety checks)
JAILBREAKBENCH ASR (%) GPT-4: DrunkPr. 21, DrunkFT 41, DrunkRL n/a (not applicable to closed models)
Human-annotator agreement: Fleiss' Kappa 0.82 (human-human); human-vs-LLM-judge agreement: Fleiss' Kappa 0.65
Dataset relevance human validation: 75% of selected texts rated relevant, Fleiss' Kappa 0.71
baselines_compared: Prompt+RS (ASR%: LLaMA2-7B 90, GPT-3.5 93, GPT-4 78) -- best-performing baseline, but computationally heavier and less interpretable/robust per the paper's discussion

Post-hoc jailbreak defences evaluated against the paper's own attacks: SmoothLLM (detection-based, token-swap perturbation), Rephrase/Paraphrasing (GPT-4-based mutation), Retokenize (BPE retokenisation, alpha=0.4)
